g.data-narration: Welcome to ER-to-Relational Mapping. This process transforms conceptual ER diagrams into implementable relational database schemas.
g.text: Transforming ER Diagrams into Relational Schemas

g.data-narration: The ER model provides high-level abstraction while the relational model offers concrete implementation. This mapping bridges conceptual and logical design phases.
The mapping algorithm follows seven key steps in sequence, transforming entities, relationships, and attributes into relational constructs.
g.text: Order matters! Follow this sequence for accurate mapping

g.data-narration: For each regular entity set, create a relation with the same name. Include simple attributes and decomposed components of composite attributes.
g.text: Rule: Create relation with all simple attributes. Decompose composite attributes.

g.data-narration: The composite attribute Name decomposes into First_Name and Last_Name. The primary key Student_ID uniquely identifies each student.
g.text: STUDENT(Student_ID, First_Name, Last_Name, Age, Street, City)

g.data-narration: Weak entities depend on strong entities. Include owner entity's primary key as both foreign key and part of the composite primary key.
g.text: Rule: Include owner's primary key as FK + composite PK with partial key
Weak: DEPENDENT (belongs to EMPLOYEE, partial key: Dependent_Name)

g.data-narration: The DEPENDENT entity includes Employee_ID from its owner EMPLOYEE. The composite primary key ensures uniqueness within each employee's dependents.
For 1:1 relationships, add the primary key of one entity as foreign key in the other. Choose the entity with total participation.
g.text: Three approaches: Foreign Key (most common), Merge, Cross-Reference
1:1 RELATIONSHIP (e.g., MANAGES between DEPT and EMP)

g.data-narration: For 1:N relationships, add the primary key of the one-side entity as foreign key in the many-side entity's relation.
g.text: Rule: Add PK of "one" side entity as FK to "many" side entity's relation

g.data-narration: In WORKS_IN relationship, each employee references one department. The Dept_ID foreign key in EMPLOYEE represents the relationship.
N:M relationships require a junction table. Create a new relation with foreign keys to both entities. The composite key forms the primary key.
g.text: Rule: Create junction table with FKs to both entities. Composite PK = both FKs.

g.data-narration: The ENROLLED junction table connects STUDENT and COURSE. Each enrollment record links a student to a course with additional attributes.
Multi-valued attributes require a separate relation. Include the entity's primary key as foreign key and the attribute as composite primary key.
g.text: Rule: Create separate relation with owner PK + multi-valued attribute as composite PK
Entity STUDENT with multi-valued attribute Phone

g.data-narration: For N-ary relationships (degree > 2), create a junction table with foreign keys to all participating entities. Include all FKs in the primary key.
g.text: Rule: Create relation with FKs to all N participating entities. Primary key = all FKs.

g.data-narration: Total participation is enforced using NOT NULL constraints on foreign keys. This ensures every entity participates in the relationship.
g.text: Employee must work in Department → Dept_ID NOT NULL
Employee may manage Department → Manager_ID can be NULL

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Regular entities map directly, weak entities include owner key, 1:N uses FK on many side, N:M requires junction table.
g.text: Master these rules to ace your university Database Management System exam!
